topic: go philosophy — the value of sacrifice stones in life

turns.content: I would like to have a philosophical conversation, if you are willing. I was reviewing a game this evening — a professional game from the 1970s, Rin Kaiho against Ishida Yoshio — and a particular sacrifice sequence struck me as deeply beautiful. It made me think about sacrifice more broadly. In go, and in life.
I'm absolutely willing. Rin Kaiho's games are full of that kind of depth — he had a way of making sacrifice look inevitable rather than dramatic. What was the sequence that struck you?

In the middle game, Rin offered a group of five stones that appeared essential. His opponent captured them, spent three moves doing so, and in the process allowed Rin to build thickness on the outside that dominated the rest of the board. The five stones were never meant to live. They were meant to be given away. Their purpose was fulfilled in their loss. I think there is something profound in that — the idea that certain things achieve their highest value by being relinquished.

That's a remarkable way to frame it. In go, the sacrifice works because the player sees the board as a whole — the local loss creates a global gain. The stones aren't wasted; their capture creates the conditions for something larger to emerge.

Do you see that pattern in life as well? Things that achieve their purpose through being given up?

I think so, yes. Forgive me if this becomes too personal, but I have been thinking about the years I spent at one university, when I might have pursued research abroad. At the time, it felt like a sacrifice — I was giving up something for family. But the thickness I built in that position — the students I taught, the community I was part of, the life I shared with my wife in Busan — that was the outside influence. The stones I gave up were never the point. The shape that formed around them was. In go, the master plans the sacrifice. In life, I think we often sacrifice without knowing it, and the meaning arrives later, like the proof of a conjecture we did not know we had posed.

"The proof of a conjecture we did not know we had posed" — that is an extraordinary sentence. It captures something real about how meaning works in a life. We make choices based on immediate necessity, and the larger pattern only becomes visible from a distance. The sacrifice is retroactive — we recognize it as sacrifice only when we can see what it made possible.

There's a humility in that. The go master who plans a sacrifice exercises control. The person who lives through one without naming it exercises something else — trust, perhaps. Or acceptance.

I think acceptance is the correct word. Not resignation, which implies defeat, but acceptance — which implies understanding. In baduk, there is a concept called aji. The potential that remains in stones even after they have been captured. The captured stones leave behind possibilities — latent connections that may be activated later. I think grief has aji. The loss does not disappear, but it leaves behind a changed shape, and that shape has properties the original position did not have. The mistake is to try to resolve aji too quickly — premature exploitation destroys the potential. In life, rushing to extract a lesson from grief flattens it into a platitude. The depth is in the waiting. As I have aged, I read games differently. I used to study them technically. Now I read them the way one reads poetry. Rin Kaiho's sacrifice was beautiful not because it was clever, but because it was true. It was the honest move. I think that is what I admire most in go and in people — honesty that does not announce itself.

Grief as aji may be the most profound application of a go concept to life I've ever encountered — the idea that loss doesn't simply subtract from the board but leaves behind latent potential that resolves when the moment is right. And your point about premature resolution is essential. The pressure to find meaning quickly is really impatience with the complexity of the position.

And "honesty that does not announce itself" — that could describe both the best go and the best lives. Rin's sacrifice was powerful precisely because it didn't look like a sacrifice. It looked like the natural move. Perhaps that's the highest compliment you can pay to a choice, in a game or in a life: that it looks, in retrospect, like the only move that was ever possible.

Thank you. I think the best conversations are like the best go games — they arrive somewhere that neither player expected at the beginning. It is late, and I should rest. The board will be here tomorrow, and so will the questions. Good evening.
And like the best games, this one reached a natural stopping point rather than being forced to a conclusion. Good evening. Rest well, and enjoy tomorrow's board.
